title: It Gets Better

jokes.text: Carlos was just tickling me — casual explanation for why George Michael is laughing, immediately deflating the narrator's grandiose 'more popular than ever' setup
jokes.setup: Narrator intones dramatically that George Michael has 'never been more popular' and is 'surrounded by people thrilled to be in his company'
jokes.punchline: George Michael laughs and explains 'Carlos was just tickling me' — the grand popularity is just a tickle fight
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator subversion — the pompous framing is immediately punctured by a mundane, goofy explanation. The contrast between the narrator's grandiosity and 'tickling me' is sharp and economical.

jokes.text: Someone saved George Michael the last root beer snow cone because 'somebody was saying that George Michael likes root beer' — a sycophantic gesture escalating into a petty fight over it
jokes.setup: George Michael has a party of people who are extremely grateful to him; someone saved him the last root beer snow cone
jokes.punchline: Carlos steals it, Terry says 'I got that for him, Carlos,' and Carlos fires back 'Teach you for being such a kiss ass, Terry' — the sycophancy collapses into infighting
jokes.explanation: The absurd specificity of 'root beer snow cone' and the sycophants turning on each other is a solid character-comedy beat. The joke is that they're so desperate to impress George Michael that they've replicated the Bluth family dysfunction in miniature.

jokes.text: 'You know my name! What's his name?' / 'You're the kiss-ass, Carlos.' — George Michael still doesn't know anyone's names; Carlos is only known because of his role label
jokes.setup: George Michael is supposedly very popular and surrounded by devoted followers
jokes.punchline: He only knows Carlos's name because Carlos is the designated kiss-ass, reducing the man to a function
jokes.explanation: Sharp little joke about social currency — George Michael can only identify people by their role, not their humanity. Well-structured and efficient.

jokes.text: 'I only know Carlos because of that old song "Carlos the Tickle Monster." / Wait, what song is that? / Oh, god, I knew he made that up.'
jokes.setup: George Michael claims he knows Carlos from 'an old song' called 'Carlos the Tickle Monster' to explain why he remembers the name
jokes.punchline: Rebel asks what song, Carlos says 'Oh god, I knew he made that up' — Carlos caught him lying in real time, but the lie is so George Michael: sweet, silly, and immediately exposed
jokes.explanation: This is a gem. The joke has three beats: the absurd specificity of 'Carlos the Tickle Monster,' Rebel's innocent question exposing the lie, and Carlos's resigned self-aware reaction. It also ties back to the opening tickling bit as a micro-callback. Perfect economy, completely character-specific — only George Michael would invent this lie and only AD would land all three beats in four lines.

jokes.text: The narrator announces 'Now the story of a family whose future was abruptly cancelled' — meta reference to the show's own cancellation
jokes.setup: Standard AD narrator intro to the episode
jokes.punchline: The word 'cancelled' refers both to the Bluth family's fortunes and to the show's actual Fox cancellation — a layered meta joke
jokes.explanation: AD's meta-awareness of its own cancellation is part of its DNA. 'Abruptly cancelled' does double duty — it's both the diegetic family story and a wink at the audience. Economy is high; the entire joke is one word doing two jobs.

jokes.text: 'It's George Michael's Arrested Development' — the title card rebrands the show as belonging to George Michael
jokes.setup: Each episode of Season 4 is told from one character's perspective
jokes.punchline: The show's title is appropriated by George Michael, suggesting both his developmental arc and a possessive claim on the series itself
jokes.explanation: Smart structural joke — the episode title format for Season 4 works on multiple levels. 'Arrested Development' can mean the show, George Michael's stunted emotional growth, or the legal situation. Economical and layered.

jokes.text: George Michael's band keeps playing after the song ends because they all sped up together — he insists it should have ended earlier, and they all argue it ended at the same time
jokes.setup: George Michael is recording a Juilliard audition demo with a band, and the song runs long
jokes.punchline: They sped up together and thus all ended simultaneously, which is technically correct but misses his point entirely; his bandmates prove they can't keep tempo by collectively drifting
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about collective incompetence — the band proves George Michael's point by being simultaneously wrong in the same direction. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Michael's Juilliard demo is for wood block, and he hopes they'll 'dig what I do so much that they start an entire department for wood block'
jokes.setup: George Michael treats his wood block playing with the utmost seriousness
jokes.punchline: He believes Juilliard will create a new department specifically for his wood block playing — the ambition is completely disproportionate to the instrument
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. The wood block as George Michael's artistic passion is perfectly calibrated — it's not the most embarrassing instrument (that would be too easy), it's just unglamorous enough to be ridiculous while he treats it with total earnestness. The Juilliard department ambition is the right escalation.

jokes.text: 'Well, then I'm not playing in a band. The whole point of the band is to showcase my amazing internal clock. You know, that's something you have to be born with.'
jokes.setup: Bandmate suggests recording George Michael separately and mixing the wood block louder
jokes.punchline: George Michael rejects the reasonable solution because the band exists purely to showcase HIS internal clock, which is absurdly self-centered for someone who seems humble
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — reveals that beneath his humility, George Michael has a secret grandiosity (the band exists to serve his tempo). The 'born with' qualifier is a nice absurdist touch that also sets up the BabyTock payoff.

jokes.text: BabyTock promotional video: 'If I were a doctor, I would guarantee you that it would also boost their brain power 110%'
jokes.setup: A promotional video for a developmental baby tool claims scientific benefits
jokes.punchline: 'If I were a doctor, I would guarantee' — the disclaimer that undermines the claim is the claim itself; 110% brain power is also an impossible metric
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of snake-oil marketing. The conditional 'if I were a doctor' does all the comedic work — it's simultaneously a legal disclaimer and a self-parody of false advertising. The 110% is a layered absurdity.

jokes.text: Lucille interrupts Tracey mid-sentence — Tracey: 'Well, that's impossible...' Lucille: 'To deny.' She has 'always wanted a smart baby.'
jokes.setup: Tracey is about to say it's impossible to claim BabyTock changed her baby's brain power
jokes.punchline: Lucille steamrolls her with 'to deny' — turning Tracey's skepticism into an endorsement — and reveals her true motivation is vanity ('I've always wanted a smart baby')
jokes.explanation: A quintessential Lucille moment: she hijacks another person's sentence to make it mean the opposite. 'I've always wanted a smart baby' lands as both vain and darkly funny — she doesn't say she wanted a smart child, she wanted a smart baby, like an accessory. Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: The BabyTock timer was invented from leftover corn-baller parts: 'The timer still works. There's no numbers on it, but it's very loud'
jokes.setup: The Bluths are stuck with 5,000 worthless corn-ballers after a failed fry-at-home venture
jokes.punchline: George Sr. salvages them by noting the timer still works — despite having no numbers — and pivots to selling them as baby products
jokes.explanation: Excellent use of the corn-baller callback. The logic of 'it works, it just has no numbers' is perfectly Bluth — technically true, fundamentally useless, sold with confidence anyway. The pivot to baby products is inspired.

jokes.text: 'Perfect for those who like the sound of a clock, but don't know their numbers.' — the narrator describes BabyTock
jokes.setup: George Sr. is trying to find a market for the numberless corn-baller timer
jokes.punchline: The narrator delivers the product positioning with dry precision — 'those who like the sound of a clock but don't know their numbers' perfectly describes babies while also exposing the absurdity of the product
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan framing is doing heavy lifting here — it describes babies in a technically accurate but absurdly clinical way. The phrase 'don't know their numbers' about infants is the kind of precision AD's narrator excels at.

jokes.text: George Sr. yells 'We'll sell this to baby— [MOTHER OF BLEEP]!' as the corn-baller burns him mid-pitch
jokes.setup: George Sr. is enthusiastically pitching the repurposed corn-baller timer
jokes.punchline: The corn-baller burns him mid-sentence — a running gag callback to the corn-baller's danger, perfectly timed to interrupt his sales pitch
jokes.explanation: The corn-baller burning someone is a classic AD running gag, but the timing here is exceptional — it interrupts George Sr. at the exact moment he's pitching the corn-baller as a safe baby product. The dramatic irony is perfect and the physical comedy lands hard.

jokes.text: 'That's not a half puny thought.' — George Sr.'s response to the baby crib idea
jokes.setup: Someone suggests putting the corn-baller timer in cribs
jokes.punchline: 'Half puny' is a malapropism or deliberate corruption of 'half-baked' and 'puny' — possibly also 'punny' — delivered with confidence as if it's a real expression
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay — 'half puny' is a funny misfire of the idiom, possibly intentional as a George Sr. character quirk. Not fully landed but gets a small recognition laugh.

jokes.text: George Michael asks his roommate to keep the Juilliard application secret from his dad 'because he still thinks of me as the same George Michael... before the world cracked me open... George Michael 2.0'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to present himself as a changed, mature adult
jokes.punchline: The self-mythology of 'George Michael 2.0' and 'the world cracked me open' is hilariously overblown for someone whose main passion is wood block and whose big transformation involved being tickled
jokes.explanation: The 'George Michael 2.0' construction is funny precisely because the upgrade is so modest. His version of transformation is playing wood block and going to Spain. The gap between his self-image and reality is the recurring engine of his arc.

jokes.text: George Michael's college send-off: the family gives him a check, Michael storms in to rip it up, demanding everyone watch him tear it apart piece by piece — 'That's it. Rip it. Couple more.'
jokes.setup: The family gives George Michael money for college as a gift; Michael arrives in a rage
jokes.punchline: Michael turns what should be a dramatic gesture of independence into a drawn-out, micromanaged destruction — he coaches the ripping ('Couple more. Did you get it? Proud of you.') which undercuts the emotional statement completely
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific cringe comedy. Michael's controlling nature is perfectly expressed: he can't even make a spontaneous gesture of defiance without micromanaging it step by step. 'Did you get it? Proud of you. Happy birthday.' is a devastating sequence — he's simultaneously furious and paternal.

jokes.text: 'Happy birthday' after ripping up the check — GOB and the family singing happy birthday as a coda to Michael's dramatic exit
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically storms out after making George Michael rip up the check
jokes.punchline: The family immediately pivots to singing Happy Birthday as if nothing happened — pure Bluth emotional compartmentalization
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash from Michael's rage-fueled pathos to 'Happy Birthday' is a classic AD move. The family's obliviousness to the emotional devastation they just witnessed is both funny and horrifying.

jokes.text: The narrator says Michael 'did make sure his son got a car' as Michael shouts 'Get out of here' and shoves him toward a car
jokes.setup: After tearing up the check (which included car money), the narrator promises Michael ensured George Michael got a car
jokes.punchline: The 'car' is Michael driving him to college and pushing him out — the narrator's framing is technically accurate but deliberately misleading
jokes.explanation: The narrator's technical-truthfulness-as-deception is an AD trademark. 'He did make sure his son got a car' setting up 'Get out of here' is a solid narrator misdirect.

jokes.text: George Michael arrives at college without any possessions — visual gag implied as the narrator says 'he arrived at college without his possessions'
jokes.setup: George Michael is going to college for a fresh start
jokes.punchline: He arrives with nothing — implicitly because Michael couldn't be bothered or the departure was too hostile to pack
jokes.explanation: A modest visual gag — the implication that he literally arrived with nothing is a consequence of Michael's dramatic scene. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Michael arrives to find something going wrong with a truck — metallic screeching, onlookers laughing — he laughs nervously saying 'N... no. No, no. What?' implying something embarrassing happened with his arrival
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at college eager to fit in
jokes.punchline: Something immediately goes wrong (implied visual gag of truck mishap), and George Michael laughs awkwardly at what is probably his own humiliation
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat of 'No, no, no... What?' is a solid cringe comedy moment. Hard to fully score without the visual, but the transcript's gaps suggest something went badly and George Michael's nervous laughter is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: George Michael's freshman-year rebellions: drinking Pepsi in the morning, having eggs at night
jokes.setup: The narrator says George Michael is 'breaking old taboos' in his freshman year
jokes.punchline: The taboos are drinking Pepsi at breakfast and having eggs at night — the deflation between 'breaking taboos' and these mild acts is the joke
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The gap between 'breaking taboos' and 'Pepsi in the morning / eggs at night' is precisely calibrated — it perfectly captures George Michael's sheltered upbringing. 'Sometimes having eggs at night' delivered with the narrator's gravitas is genuinely funny. The specificity (Pepsi, not soda; eggs, not something edgier) is the kind of detail that elevates AD above its peers.

jokes.text: George Michael overhears himself telling a joke — '(laughing): I forgot it wasn't morning' — and we hear it getting laughs from the original scene and then his friends laughing at the retelling
jokes.setup: He accidentally ordered breakfast food at night and made a self-deprecating comment
jokes.punchline: The scene briefly replays the line as if it's his greatest social triumph — 'he was finding others enjoyed his sense of humor' — which recontextualizes a tiny, forgettable joke as a life-changing moment
jokes.explanation: Layered joke about social isolation — for George Michael, getting a laugh at 'I forgot it wasn't morning' is apparently a social breakthrough. The fact that this minor quip is treated as a highlight of his freshman year tells us everything about how lonely he was.

jokes.text: GOB's magic present 'elevated his newfound confidence' then immediately 'diminishing it' — visual gag implied of a magic trick gone wrong
jokes.setup: GOB sends George Michael a present — presumably a magic trick — as a confidence boost
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes it elevated then immediately diminished his confidence — implying the trick humiliated him in front of people he was trying to impress
jokes.explanation: A quick implied gag about GOB's reliably counterproductive generosity. The narrator's structure ('elevated... diminishing it') does the comedic heavy lifting in the absence of a visual.

jokes.text: Sophomore year montage: 'everything got turned up to 11' — followed by a highlight reel of events including Ray constantly reminding him about Becky
jokes.setup: Sophomore year is narrated as a period of intensity
jokes.punchline: Ray appears twice in the same montage to remind George Michael about his breakup with Becky — 'Oh thanks, Ray, I almost forgot' — and then Ray says 'You know who I was thinking about before you sat down? Becky. Do you remember?'
jokes.explanation: Ray as an oblivious wound-reopener is a compact running gag. The escalation — first time it's painful, second time Ray immediately brings Becky up again after apparently forgetting he just did — is tight. 'Oh thanks, Ray, I almost forgot' is a good line.

jokes.text: George Michael volunteers for a kissing science experiment — discovers what the straps are for when a mechanical 'mouth unit' is brought in instead of a human
jokes.setup: George Michael signs up for a kissing study between 'a man and a woman,' presumably hoping to kiss a real person
jokes.punchline: 'Bringing in the mouth unit!' — it's a machine, not a person; the straps were to hold him in place for a mechanical kissing device
jokes.explanation: Sharp misdirection — the entire setup suggests a human kissing study, and 'Bringing in the mouth unit!' is a great reveal. The subsequent letters (C, M, Z, N, F) that all score '1' suggest increasingly bizarre mechanical mouth configurations. Strong cringe comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael's year abroad: he was nannying children for a woman who 'opened him up sexually'
jokes.setup: George Michael spent a year abroad trying 'not to be an imposing guest in his new country'
jokes.punchline: The woman whose children he was nannying 'opened him up sexually' — the juxtaposition of nannying and sexual awakening is the joke
jokes.explanation: The narrator's clinical 'opened him up sexually' after the wholesome nannying description is a solid tonal contrast. Sets up the Spanish sequence.

jokes.text: 'Hey, hey, did Ernesto get his 4:00?' / 'The drones are coming. Nothing matters now.' — interrupting the sexual awakening scene
jokes.setup: The Spanish woman has just begun 'opening up' George Michael — a romantically charged moment
jokes.punchline: Someone abruptly asks about 'Ernesto's 4:00' and another person announces 'The drones are coming. Nothing matters now.' — existential despair interrupts the seduction
jokes.explanation: 'The drones are coming. Nothing matters now.' is a genuinely weird absurdist line that earns its laugh through sheer non-sequitur confidence. The tonal collision between erotic awakening and bureaucratic despair is funny.

jokes.text: 'Why do you kiss like this?' / 'The teeth, they must not be used like this.' — the Spanish woman critiques George Michael's kissing technique; narrator notes 'it was actually a jaw and lip retraction issue but she had neither a command of the language nor the data that we do'
jokes.setup: The Spanish woman is correcting George Michael's terrible kissing
jokes.punchline: The narrator defends George Michael with 'she had neither a command of the language nor the data that we do' — the narrator taking George Michael's side against his own sex tutor is absurd, and the clinical language ('jaw and lip retraction issue') vs. her romantic frustration is the joke
jokes.explanation: The narrator being partisan on George Michael's behalf — smugly defending his kissing technique in clinical terms — is a great subversion of the narrator's usual omniscient remove. 'She had neither a command of the language nor the data that we do' is an excellent line. The implied data source for kissing quality is never explained, adding to the absurdity.

jokes.text: 'You make love like a boy.' / 'Spanish, speak to me in Spanish.' — George Michael's response to being sexually criticized is to ask for Spanish because college credit requires it
jokes.setup: The Spanish woman criticizes George Michael's lovemaking
jokes.punchline: George Michael's response is not to improve but to ask her to speak Spanish — 'because the only way that college credit is actually activated is if I have...' — revealing he's thinking about academic requirements mid-seduction
jokes.explanation: Outstanding character comedy. When told 'you make love like a boy,' George Michael's instinct is to redirect to language requirements for college credit — revealing that even in his most overtly sexual moment, he's still a diligent student. The joke is perfect because it's simultaneously deflating and completely in character. 'Because the only way that college credit is actually activated' is a brilliant line. High on all craft metrics.

jokes.text: 'Oh, yes, make me cry.' / 'You're a terrible mother.' / (high-pitched crying)
jokes.setup: The Spanish woman says 'make me cry' as an erotic command
jokes.punchline: George Michael tells her 'You're a terrible mother' — interpreting the request literally and finding the most available insult, which happens to be true (she left her kids with a nanny), causing her to actually cry
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The structure is flawless: a conventional erotic request ('make me cry') is literalized by George Michael in the worst possible way ('You're a terrible mother'), which lands because (a) it's exactly what a sheltered, literalist George Michael would say, (b) it's arguably true since she left her kids with a nanny to seduce him, and (c) it works. The high-pitched crying confirms it. Economy is perfect — four words and a crying sound effect. This is easily an 8.5+ joke.

jokes.text: George Michael returns to senior year 'eager to unleash his new overt sexuality on an unsuspecting campus' — walks in, says 'It gets better,' gets laughs
jokes.setup: After his sexual awakening in Spain, George Michael is ready to reinvent himself
jokes.punchline: He walks in and says 'It gets better' — which is an LGBT empowerment phrase but here is used to mean 'things will improve' — and it gets laughs from the crowd, implying they're laughing at him, not with him
jokes.explanation: The episode title lands here as both the show's thesis and a joke about George Michael's awkward charisma. The people laughing are laughing at his delivery, not his charm — which makes 'it gets better' ironic for him personally.

jokes.text: The narrator says he was 'like a king returning to his castle' at the same moment George Michael says 'I sort of feel like a king returning to his castle'
jokes.setup: George Michael returns to his dorm for senior year
jokes.punchline: The narrator and George Michael say almost the exact same thing simultaneously — the narrator is either directly sampling George Michael's thoughts or George Michael has absorbed the narrator's clichés
jokes.explanation: A clever meta-joke about the relationship between the AD narrator and the characters. The synchronization implies either the narrator is in George Michael's head or George Michael thinks in clichés. Either reading is funny and character-specific.

jokes.text: The roommates think 'George Michael' means two people named 'Michael' and 'George' — 'Can't wait to meet Michael, George'
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at his double-size castle expecting a solo room
jokes.punchline: His roommates think 'George Michael' is two people — they've doubled the room size and are expecting two tenants named George and Michael
jokes.explanation: A solid name joke that plays on the fact that 'George Michael' is a famous person's name — the roommates apparently didn't realize it's one person. The doubled room is a nice escalation of the misunderstanding.

jokes.text: Michael arrives at college: 'Pete died.' / 'Oh, God. Who's, uh, Pete?' / 'Don't worry about it.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at George Michael's dorm unannounced; George Michael prepares to 'unveil George Michael 2.0'
jokes.punchline: Michael's opener is 'Pete died' — a person so inconsequential to George Michael that he doesn't know who Pete is; Michael dismisses the death immediately with 'Don't worry about it'
jokes.explanation: Sharp economy joke. 'Pete died. / Who's Pete? / Don't worry about it.' is three lines that accomplish a lot — Michael leads with bad news about a stranger, George Michael shows appropriate alarm, and Michael immediately deprioritizes the death. It's very funny and very Bluth: death is a footnote if it doesn't serve the immediate agenda.

jokes.text: Michael sees the mustache and says 'Probably gonna want to shave it off before the start of next quarter. Otherwise they're gonna think you're a musician, huh?' — immediately undermining George Michael's new identity
jokes.setup: George Michael has grown a mustache as part of his new overt identity; Michael says he 'loves' it
jokes.punchline: Michael pivots to 'probably gonna want to shave it off' — his approval is immediately conditional and controlling, and the reasoning ('they'll think you're a musician') unknowingly attacks George Michael's actual aspiration
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific joke. Michael's conditional approval — loving something then immediately planning its removal — is his controlling nature in miniature. The dramatic irony that 'they'll think you're a musician' is the exact thing George Michael aspires to be makes it even richer.

jokes.text: 'That sounds like a mother of an invention.' — George Michael's response to 'too bad there's no such thing as an electric wood block'
jokes.setup: P-Hound says there's no such thing as an electric wood block
jokes.punchline: 'That sounds like a mother of an invention' — a pun on 'necessity is the mother of invention' delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: A solid pun — 'mother of an invention' riffs on the proverb while also echoing 'Mother of [bleep]' from the corn-baller scene. Pleasant wordplay, doesn't overstay its welcome.

jokes.text: The naming process for the app: Woodblock (taken), Blockwood (taken), Block of Wood (taken), Block-block (no — 'people are going to think we're in the chicken-noise business')
jokes.setup: They need a name for their wood block app and systematically go through options
jokes.punchline: 'Block-block' is rejected because people will think they're in the chicken-noise business — a completely nonsensical association that P-Hound demonstrates ('it's kind of hard to do in these matador pants')
jokes.explanation: The naming escalation is great — each iteration is rejected for a funnier reason, culminating in the completely absurd 'chicken-noise business' objection. 'It's kind of hard to do in these matador pants' is a wonderful grace note.

jokes.text: Fakeblock domain name is available for $5,000; George Michael just spent 99 cents on competitor apps — '...times three'
jokes.setup: They need to register 'Fakeblock' as a domain name
jokes.punchline: 'It's $5,000 for the domain name' / 'I got the last [root beer snow cone]. That was 99 cents.' / 'Times three.'
jokes.explanation: 'Times three' lands as a running gag call-back — the 99-cent app downloads have become a recurring punchline about their financial naivety. The contrast with the $5,000 domain name is perfectly structured.

jokes.text: Tutoring agency: 'Is this "F" in "Maeby F." her last name or her grade?' / 'Oh, it's her last name. But her most recent grade is... well, this just shows an Elvis Presley head.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is applying to be a tutor and reviews a student file for 'Maeby F.'
jokes.punchline: He asks if 'F' is her grade, which it plausibly is; the employee clarifies it's her last name but then reveals her actual grade is represented by 'an Elvis Presley head' — worse than an F
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup-punchline. The question 'is that her grade or her last name?' is a perfect setup because the answer is technically 'last name' but then is immediately undercut by 'her most recent grade is... an Elvis Presley head.' The Elvis Presley head as a grade below F is an absurdist touch that's specific, original, and quotable. The pause before the reveal ('well, this just shows...') is great structural storytelling.

jokes.text: 'So, that bad? / I'll take her. / Stay on top of her. / You may need to ride her pretty hard.'
jokes.setup: The tutoring agency is describing how much attention Maeby needs as a student
jokes.punchline: The tutoring euphemisms ('stay on top of her,' 'ride her pretty hard') are innocent in professional context but George Michael's interest in Maeby makes them accidentally suggestive
jokes.explanation: A fairly standard double-entendre, relying on obvious sexual euphemisms in an academic context. The craft is lower than most AD jokes because this type of joke (innocent phrase, dirty implication) is common sitcom fare. Gets a laugh but isn't particularly original.

jokes.text: George Michael's roommate is also interested in Maeby — he's wearing the same shirt as George Michael and Maeby compliments it on him, causing George Michael to realize he's been betrayed
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to set up a seduction scenario with Maeby; his roommate is supposed to help
jokes.punchline: The roommate is wearing the same shirt George Michael bought to impress Maeby, and she compliments HIM on it — the wingman has stolen the look
jokes.explanation: A solid betrayal beat — the narrator's running label of 'betrayal' as a chapter heading gets a payoff here. The shirt theft is a petty but recognizable form of backstabbing.

jokes.text: George Michael confesses to Maeby about their past relationship: 'for whatever reason, I listened to my dad who said we shouldn't make it work.' / 'Was she your first?' / 'Cousin? No.' / 'Why would your mind go right to... ? No.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is explaining why he and Maeby broke up
jokes.punchline: P-Hound asks 'Was she your first?' and George Michael immediately interprets it as 'cousin' — revealing his own guilty conscience about the cousin angle before P-Hound even implies it
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's finest moments. P-Hound simply asks 'Was she your first?' — clearly meaning 'girlfriend' — and George Michael immediately says 'Cousin? No.' He interprets the most neutral possible question as an accusation about incest, which exposes his own guilt perfectly. The follow-up 'why would your mind go right to... ?' is perfect because it's exactly what anyone would say after that bizarre non-sequitur. Only works with this specific character and this specific history. Exceptional character integration and structure.

jokes.text: George Michael says 'I wasn't yet O.S.' — P-Hound: 'Overtly sexual.' — George Michael: 'Overtly sexual. Right. As I live and breathe. Crazy. That's just crazy.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is explaining why he didn't pursue Maeby — he wasn't overtly sexual yet
jokes.punchline: P-Hound knows what 'O.S.' means (overtly sexual), confirming it's George Michael's actual acronym for himself; George Michael repeats 'as I live and breathe, crazy' — setting up a phrase he'll deploy awkwardly later
jokes.explanation: The acronym 'O.S.' for 'overtly sexual' is a funny character detail — George Michael has systematized his sexual self-improvement. Sets up the later callback when he tries to deploy 'as I live and breathe' as a seduction line.

jokes.text: George Michael rehearses the fake-coincidence meeting with Maeby: 'Maeby, what are you doing here? As I live and breathe.' — narrator: 'But for the phrase, he nailed it.'
jokes.setup: George Michael wants this to 'look like fate' when Maeby arrives; he rehearses the encounter
jokes.punchline: The narrator's verdict is that he nailed the encounter 'but for the phrase' — meaning everything was good except the weird 'as I live and breathe' line, which is a wildly archaic expression that betrayed the naturalness he needed
jokes.explanation: The narrator's precise verdict — 'but for the phrase, he nailed it' — is perfectly calibrated. It acknowledges partial success while making clear that 'as I live and breathe' was so weird it tanked the whole thing. The understatement is elegant.

jokes.text: 'Don't you need an education?' / 'Don't forget... I'm a Harris. Someone who "inharris" a lot of money.'
jokes.setup: George Michael challenges Maeby on her strategy of staying in high school forever instead of getting an education
jokes.punchline: Maeby invents 'inharris' as a portmanteau of 'inherits' and her last name 'Harris' — a self-serving wordplay that she delivers with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: Maeby's verbal dexterity is perfectly captured — she invents a word, deploys it confidently, and moves on. 'Someone who inharris a lot of money' is a good Maeby line that captures her specific brand of confident bullshitting.

jokes.text: 'I wouldn't put all my Anns in that basket.' — George Michael's warning to Maeby about over-relying on inheritance
jokes.setup: George Michael is warning Maeby not to rely solely on inherited money
jokes.punchline: 'Don't put all your eggs in one basket' becomes 'all my Anns in that basket' — Ann Veal being George Michael's ex-girlfriend, making this a deeply personal and layered pun
jokes.explanation: This is a spectacular layered joke. 'Don't put all your eggs in one basket' is rewritten as 'don't put all my Anns in that basket' — where Ann Veal replaces 'eggs,' suggesting she's been filed away with other interchangeable things, AND it's a dig at Ann's blandness (Anns are fungible). The fact that George Michael delivers this to Maeby while trying to seduce her — when Ann was his consolation girlfriend — makes it even richer. Exceptional character integration and economy.

jokes.text: George Michael claims he 'made a 40-year-old woman cry in Spain' as evidence of his overt sexuality; P-Hound responds 'I love making love to Spanish women'; George Michael says 'Get out.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to claim his sexual credentials after failing to seduce Maeby
jokes.punchline: His sexual achievement is making a woman cry with an insult (not passion), which P-Hound misunderstands as impressive; George Michael's 'Get out' is a flustered rejection of P-Hound's unsolicited story
jokes.explanation: A solid callback to the Spain sequence — George Michael's 'making her cry' is referenced as sexual prowess when it was actually an accidental insult. The misunderstanding layers nicely.

jokes.text: 'A man, a woman... a bedroom. Let's solve for X.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to pivot back to seduction after the Maeby conversation
jokes.punchline: He uses an algebra metaphor for sex — 'let's solve for X' — which is both clever and revealing of his inability to actually be overtly sexual
jokes.explanation: A very good George Michael joke. 'Let's solve for X' as a seduction line is perfect — it tries to be charming and ends up being sincere, nerdy, and slightly effective. The delivery (A MAN, A WOMAN... a bedroom.) with the dramatic pause before the math punchline is excellent structure.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'X isn't a number.' — George Michael cannot resist correcting her even while trying to seduce her
jokes.setup: George Michael uses 'let's solve for X' as a seduction line
jokes.punchline: Maeby says 'X isn't a number' — she's technically wrong (X is a variable that represents a number), and George Michael cannot stop himself from correcting her
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Maeby's casual error triggers George Michael's teaching instinct, which is stronger than his libido. 'So you don't even really know the basic...' followed by the narrator noting 'the urge to educate her was more powerful than the urge to kiss her' is a perfect summary of George Michael's character. Excellent structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'The urge to educate her was more powerful than the urge to kiss her.' — George Michael drops the seduction to teach Maeby algebra
jokes.setup: George Michael is mid-seduction
jokes.punchline: He abandons the romantic moment to show Maeby how algebra works: '3X+1=10...'
jokes.explanation: The narrator's line is the punchline to the algebra setup — 'the urge to educate her was more powerful than the urge to kiss her' is a beautiful summary. Then the scene pays it off by having him actually write '3X+1=10.' The character consistency is impeccable.

jokes.text: After the algebra lesson, George Michael tries to return to the seduction: 'Therefore, when I say, "You and me in a bedroom, let's solve for X," I'm... I'm stating it as a kind of formula.' / 'Yeah, let's get wasted. Let's drink that formula.'
jokes.setup: George Michael taught Maeby algebra, then tries to return to his seduction metaphor
jokes.punchline: Maeby completely misunderstands 'formula' and suggests drinking it — she's extracted zero understanding from the lesson, confirming the educational detour was pointless
jokes.explanation: The callback lands well — George Michael spent all this time teaching algebra to set up his seduction metaphor, and Maeby heard 'formula' and thought of a beverage. Her response also captures her fundamental disinterest in learning. 'No, I'll show you what a formula is real quick, 'cause that's very important' as he misses the window entirely is excellent.

jokes.text: George Michael's interior monologue about whether to perpetuate the Fakeblock lie — 41 seconds pass according to his 'unfailing internal clock'
jokes.setup: George Michael is deciding whether to lie about having privacy software to impress Maeby
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes 'He had not responded now for 41 seconds, according to his unfailing internal clock' — his silence is precisely measured by the wood-block talent established throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: A lovely structural callback — the internal clock joke from BabyTock pays off here. The fact that George Michael's defining musical talent (his internal clock) manifests during his worst social failure (standing silent for 41 seconds while failing to lie or tell the truth) is perfect character irony.

jokes.text: After 41 seconds, George Michael begins lying about Fakeblock: 'A Boolean-driven aggregation, really, of what programmers call hacker traps' — the narrator: 'he found himself suddenly and effortlessly tapping into a long-inbred instinct for lying. A Bluth taking his first steps in deceit.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been trying to be honest; now he decides to lie about having privacy software
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes it as 'tapping into a long-inbred instinct for lying. A Bluth taking his first steps in deceit.' — framing his lie as a genetic awakening and a coming-of-age milestone
jokes.explanation: The framing of George Michael's first significant lie as a biological Bluth inheritance — 'long-inbred instinct for lying' — is excellent. It positions him within the family mythology while also being funny on its own. 'A Bluth taking his first steps in deceit' is a great line with genuine weight.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And he found himself suddenly and effortlessly tapping into a long-inbred instinct for lying. A Bluth taking his first steps in deceit.'
jokes.setup: George Michael decides to lie about Fakeblock being real privacy software
jokes.punchline: The narrator frames this as a hereditary milestone — 'long-inbred instinct for lying' — and 'A Bluth taking his first steps in deceit' presents family-brand dishonesty as a developmental achievement
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest narrator lines. 'Long-inbred instinct for lying' does triple duty: (1) it's a callback to the Bluth family's established dishonesty, (2) 'inbred' carries incest connotations given the George Michael/Maeby situation, (3) the phrase 'first steps in deceit' frames lying as a developmental milestone — darkly parental. Character integration is near-perfect because this could only be about a Bluth. Very quotable.

jokes.text: 'Ay, dios mio.' — George Michael's Spanish reaction to realizing Fakeblock could be bigger than Facebook
jokes.setup: The conversation reveals Fakeblock could be a genuinely massive company
jokes.punchline: George Michael's only contribution to the stunned silence is 'Ay, dios mio' — a reflexive Spanish phrase, signaling that his 'George Michael 2.0' persona is so new that Spanish is now his go-to for strong emotions
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character beat — the Spanish interjection reveals how thin the George Michael 2.0 veneer is. He's using Spanish as a personality prop.

jokes.text: 'No. No, of course not. No. I mean, this is my chance with Maeby, and I can't even get rid of my own father.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks if George Michael can really build the world-changing privacy software he just described
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately breaks and admits he can't — his concern is not the software but getting Michael to leave so he can pursue Maeby
jokes.explanation: Good honesty beat — the gap between the world-changing technology and 'I can't even get rid of my own father' is perfect George Michael. His real problem is always personal, never professional.

jokes.text: George Michael's elaborate plan to get Michael to vote himself out: 'Not if I get him to vote against himself.' / 'Why would he do that?' / 'No, he wouldn't. He... I was just leaning back, so it looked like I...' / 'I got this chair again, didn't I?'
jokes.setup: George Michael proposes an elaborate scheme to vote Michael out of the dorm
jokes.punchline: He trails off mid-explanation when he realizes he was just leaning back in a chair — he has no actual plan; 'I got this chair again, didn't I?' reveals this is a recurring thing where the chair gives him false confidence
jokes.explanation: The chair-induced false confidence is a lovely absurdist character detail. 'I got this chair again, didn't I?' suggests George Michael has a recurring problem with one specific chair making him feel smarter than he is. Very specific and funny.

jokes.text: The privacy software pitch becomes 'Sounds like you guys are working on your privacy software, huh?' — Michael interprets the dorm voting conversation as privacy software development
jokes.setup: George Michael and P-Hound are discussing how to vote Michael out; Michael overhears
jokes.punchline: Michael misinterprets 'I'm putting up a wall, we need to rework our whole system here' as technical privacy software talk — confirming the lie is already self-sustaining
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed dramatic irony moment — the language of privacy and walls maps onto real dorm-room politics, so Michael hears his son's Fakeblock pitch in a mundane conversation. The lie sustains itself accidentally.

jokes.text: 'And it's, "Adios, brothiero." / It cannot fail.'
jokes.setup: The plan to vote Michael out of the dorm succeeds
jokes.punchline: 'Brothiero' is George Michael's attempt at a Spanish/Italian farewell — his newly minted multilingualism is as fake as his software company
jokes.explanation: 'Brothiero' — not a word in any language — captures George Michael's strained cosmopolitanism. The confidence with which he says 'It cannot fail' is also funny given what we've seen of his plans.

jokes.text: 'Did he look betrayed to you?' / 'He kind of always looks that way.'
jokes.setup: After voting Michael out, George Michael worries about his father's feelings
jokes.punchline: P-Hound notes Michael 'kind of always looks that way' — Michael's default expression is betrayed, making it impossible to tell when he's actually hurt
jokes.explanation: A sharp character observation about Michael Bluth that's completely accurate — his martyred expression is his default setting, making genuine hurt invisible. 'He kind of always looks that way' is a very quotable line that doubles as series-level commentary on Michael.

jokes.text: Maeby is getting a 'lifetime achievement award' at a gala — she's still in high school
jokes.setup: George Michael asks if Maeby can blow off a thing she has in L.A.
jokes.punchline: It's a lifetime achievement award — Maeby, a current high school student, is receiving a lifetime achievement award
jokes.explanation: A great Maeby joke — her impersonation of a film executive is so successful she's receiving lifetime achievement awards as a teenager. The absurdity is perfect Arrested Development and completely character-specific.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I can't bring any of my friends without getting permission slips from their parents.' — she's an adult receiving lifetime achievement awards but needs parental permission for her friends to attend
jokes.setup: Maeby needs a plus-one to the gala but all her peers are high-school age
jokes.punchline: She can't bring her high school friends to her lifetime achievement award gala without parental permission slips — the juxtaposition of professional achievement and childhood logistics
jokes.explanation: The permission slip joke is a perfect distillation of the Maeby paradox — she's a Hollywood executive and simultaneously a minor. 'I can't bring any of my friends without getting permission slips from their parents' lands because the logic is absolutely correct and completely absurd.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, 'cause you just betrayed yours, right?' — Maeby's casual observation that George Michael voted his dad out
jokes.setup: George Michael offers to be Maeby's plus-one since he doesn't need permission from parents
jokes.punchline: Maeby casually notes it's because he betrayed his — the show's chapter heading theme ('Betrayal') is deployed as a throwaway observation
jokes.explanation: Maeby's casual 'you just betrayed yours, right?' is a sharp observation delivered with perfect nonchalance. The fact that it's her instinctive understanding of the situation — and she thinks it's cool — tells us everything about Maeby's moral framework.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Rebel 'In Spain, they don't even eat dinner until after 11 p.m.' as if it's a profound personal revelation
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to seem worldly at the gala
jokes.punchline: The most exotic thing he can share about Spain is meal timing — his 'transformation' is superficial cultural trivia
jokes.explanation: A functional character joke — 'in Spain, they don't eat dinner until 11' is the kind of factoid someone shares when they want to seem cultured but haven't processed their experiences. Tying back to his eggs-at-night freshman revelation.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I know this guy. Hey.' / 'Oh. It's Justin. That's David Henrie. He's Justin from Wizards of Waverly Place.' / 'Hmm.' / 'It's on a lot in Spain.'
jokes.setup: George Michael sees someone at the gala and wants to seem connected
jokes.punchline: The person is David Henrie from 'Wizards of Waverly Place' — a Disney Channel show — and George Michael only knows him because 'it's on a lot in Spain,' undermining his attempts at adult sophistication
jokes.explanation: The meta-casting of David Henrie (a real actor from a Disney show) as himself, recognized by George Michael from a children's program 'on a lot in Spain,' is a good joke. It deflates his worldliness — he watched Spanish Disney Channel.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I'm going to go mingle with a bunch of 13-year-olds who think they can still play fifth grade.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is at a gala where she's receiving a lifetime achievement award
jokes.punchline: Her peers at this Hollywood event are 13-year-olds she treats as fellow frauds — she mingles with child actors who are 'pretending' to play fifth graders, which mirrors her own deception of pretending to be a student
jokes.explanation: Maeby's phrasing — '13-year-olds who think they can still play fifth grade' — is a knowing dig at young actors while also being a self-referential joke about her own pretense. 'Think they can still play fifth grade' implies she sees through everyone's fakery, making her a perfect character for this scene. Strong Maeby voice.

jokes.text: George Michael at the hotel check-in: 'Buenas noches, pequeño Scarlet. I will be needing a room, please. / How many nights? / Two. Sorry. No, just one night. / I thought you were saying how many people. / And how many beds? / Two. / No. Uh, one bed. / Why give her the option? Keep it real simple. One bed, one night, two people, let's solve for X.' (laughs) / 'I hate young Hollywood.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to book a hotel room for himself and Maeby, attempting to be suave
jokes.punchline: He fumbles the check-in completely — wrong number of nights, wrong number of beds — then pivots to his seduction line ('let's solve for X'), which lands so poorly the clerk says 'I hate young Hollywood'
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy with a great callback. George Michael's suave Spanish opener ('Buenas noches, pequeño Scarlet') immediately collapses into confused bed-counting. The clerk's deadpan 'I hate young Hollywood' is a perfect button — she's judging him as a pretentious entertainment-industry kid, not knowing he's just a confused undergrad.

jokes.text: Maeby is pitching Fakeblock to investors at the gala: 'It's called Fakeblock, Mort. It's going to blow Schnoodle out of the water!'
jokes.setup: George Michael finds Maeby at the gala pitching business
jokes.punchline: Maeby is already pitching Fakeblock — the fake software that doesn't exist — to real investors, showing her natural talent for selling things that aren't real (consistent with her film executive persona)
jokes.explanation: Maeby pitching the fake software at a professional gala is a perfect escalation of the Fakeblock lie and completely in character for her. She's been faking her way through the film industry for years; Fakeblock is just her latest project.

jokes.text: 'How does your privacy software work?' / 'Huh? It's private.'
jokes.setup: Rebel asks George Michael (as George Maharis) how his privacy software works
jokes.punchline: 'It's private.' — the perfect non-answer that is also technically a tautology: privacy software that operates privately
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. Three words: 'Huh? It's private.' The setup is a reasonable technical question; the answer is a complete non-answer that is simultaneously a perfect deflection and a logical tautology (privacy software's workings are private). Economy is maximum — you literally cannot say less while saying more. Highly quotable, structurally perfect.

jokes.text: George Michael gives his name as 'George Maharis' then immediately hears it paged: 'Is George Michael here? Is George Michael here?' — and laughs 'Oh, God. Just remind me not to go in the men's room!'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been using the alias 'George Maharis'; his real name is paged over the intercom
jokes.punchline: He laughs and says 'remind me not to go in the men's room' — an oblique reference to a public men's room cruising joke (or possibly a restroom incident), completely non-sequitur to Rebel
jokes.explanation: A slightly obscure joke — 'George Michael' being paged triggers some anxiety about the men's room (possibly a reference to the real George Michael's arrest). The joke works on that subtext level for viewers who get it.

jokes.text: Rebel is dating an 'undercover cop boyfriend' she's trying to get into bed — George Michael just booked a hotel room for one night with one bed for himself and Maeby
jokes.setup: George Michael has maneuvered himself into an impressive position as 'George Maharis' and feels momentum
jokes.punchline: 'That's my undercover cop boyfriend. I'm trying to get him in bed.' / 'Boyfriend? / So funny that didn't come up in the car.'
jokes.explanation: 'So funny that didn't come up in the car' is a great line — understated, precise, and perfectly captures the frustration of a romantic setup that immediately goes wrong. The undercover cop detail also sets up future plot complications.

jokes.text: John Beard's news broadcast: 'Fakeblock's mystery man, George Maharis, may have just found a way to screw a cap on the Internet, and investors are lining up to fill the company's tank with money.' / 'That's all the news that's fit to pump. I quit.'
jokes.setup: Fakeblock gets news coverage after the accidental cyber-sabotage of Schnoodle
jokes.punchline: John Beard delivers an extended car-metaphor news report then signs off with 'That's all the news that's fit to pump. I quit.' — a riff on 'all the news that's fit to print' (New York Times motto)
jokes.explanation: John Beard's oddly themed 'car metaphor' news segment capped with 'I quit' is a solid running gag deployment. The extended metaphor (screw a cap, fill the tank, fit to pump) is committed bit-work. 'I quit' as a tag is a nice recurring Beard joke.

jokes.text: P-Hound upon learning George Michael IS George Maharis: 'Why would you do that?' / 'You know, it's like the time you told that girl you were George Takei's son.'
jokes.setup: George Michael confesses he invented Fakeblock to impress Maeby
jokes.punchline: P-Hound immediately draws a parallel to a time he told a girl he was George Takei's son — an equally absurd lie that reveals they both have a pattern of ridiculous deceptions for romantic purposes
jokes.explanation: P-Hound's 'George Takei's son' parallel is a great joke — it normalizes George Michael's deception by showing P-Hound has done equivalently absurd things for similar reasons. The specificity of George Takei is funny, and the casual way P-Hound equates the two situations is perfect.

jokes.text: 'I put in the first three dollars, so that makes you a hop-on.' / 'No, you're the hop-on, Paul.' / 'I know exactly who you're trying to hop on to.' / (both) 'Maeby.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and P-Hound argue over who is the real founder of Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: 'Hop-on' (a Bluth term for someone who attaches themselves to another's project) becomes a double entendre — P-Hound is also 'hopping on' Maeby romantically, which they both know
jokes.explanation: The 'hop-on' callback combined with the double meaning (business/romantic) is a good AD-style layered joke. The simultaneous 'Maeby' as a reveal confirms they both know exactly what's happening.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I trust you, George Michael. I would not get into bed with you on this thing if I didn't trust you.' / 'No. Yeah, of... of course you can trust me in bed.' / 'No, I mean, I trust you not to take advantage of me.' / 'No. I wouldn't. We would just lie there.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is explaining she trusts George Michael with the Fakeblock business investment
jokes.punchline: George Michael interprets every business bed-metaphor literally, then when Maeby clarifies she means business trust, he says 'We would just lie there' — completely missing the point while confirming his romantic intentions
jokes.explanation: An exceptional cringe comedy exchange. The business/romantic double meaning of 'get into bed' is handled with perfect structure: Maeby uses it as pure metaphor, George Michael takes it literally, Maeby clarifies, and George Michael's 'We would just lie there' reveals both his restraint AND his romantic thoughts. The cousin subtext makes 'we would just lie there' land with extra weight. Could only happen with these two specific characters.

jokes.text: 'We'd just lie there? I mean, I'm talking about business.' / 'So am I. That's why we would just lie there.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is confused by George Michael's 'we would just lie there' response
jokes.punchline: George Michael clarifies he's also talking about business — and apparently in a business context, lying there is appropriate. The logic makes no sense which is exactly the point: he's so flustered he's now applying romantic behavior to business
jokes.explanation: The follow-up lands harder than the setup. 'So am I. That's why we would just lie there.' — George Michael has now inverted the metaphor completely. It's perfect cringe logic: he starts interpreting business language as romance, then interprets his romantic response as business. His brain has completely short-circuited.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'We'd just lie there? I mean, I'm talking about business.' / George Michael: 'So am I. That's why we would just lie there.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to clarify she meant business, not literal bed
jokes.punchline: George Michael confirms he's talking about business too — which means his 'we would just lie there' is a description of his business strategy (passive, deceptive). The double meaning of 'lie' (lying in bed / lying to investors) crystallizes perfectly
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and among the finest moments in AD's wordplay canon. 'That's why we would just lie there' works on four simultaneous levels: (1) literal — he's imagining lying in bed with Maeby, (2) romantic — he's describing a scenario of restrained desire with his cousin, (3) business — they will literally just 'lie' (deceive) to investors about Fakeblock, (4) character — George Michael's fundamental passivity and dishonesty expressed in six words. Economy score 9.5 because it does more per word than almost anything in the episode. Structure is near-perfect: the setup in joke #71 makes this payoff land like a gut punch. Rewatch bonus because you catch new layers on every viewing.

jokes.text: 'When you become an Internet billionaire, you could do whatever you want with me in bed.' — Maeby's offhand comment that makes George Michael change his mind about the lie
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to reassure George Michael about the business partnership
jokes.punchline: Maeby accidentally says the one thing that would make George Michael abandon his plan to come clean — the suggestion of a future bed arrangement. The narrator confirms: 'It was that. That's what made him change his mind.'
jokes.explanation: The narrator's explicit confirmation 'It was that. That's what made him change his mind' is a perfect structural button. Maeby accidentally triggers exactly the wrong response from George Michael by casually mentioning a bed scenario. The joke is that his entire moral framework collapses in the face of a completely offhand comment.

jokes.text: GOB tries to meet George Michael at a 'magic club' — but takes him to what appears to be a gay club — supposedly to tell him his father is upset
jokes.setup: GOB says 'I need to see you tonight. It's about your father and you.' George Michael thinks it's urgent
jokes.punchline: GOB takes him to a 'magic club' (which may double as a gay club based on Season 4 GOB storylines) to deliver the simple message 'your dad is really, really, really upset with you'
jokes.explanation: GOB's circuitous communication — inviting George Michael somewhere urgent to deliver a message George Michael literally asked about at the start — is a solid GOB character beat. The venue choice adds dimension for viewers tracking GOB's Season 4 arc.

jokes.text: 'He is upset? I asked you that. That was literally the first thing I asked you. So you're giving me these crazy... Mad?'
jokes.setup: GOB finally tells George Michael his dad is upset, after making him come to a magic club
jokes.punchline: George Michael points out that 'is he upset' was literally the first thing he asked GOB — GOB made him travel to a venue to answer the original question
jokes.explanation: A good GOB-specific joke about his inability to communicate directly. The frustrated 'I asked you that' is a clean character moment showing how dealing with GOB wears people down.

jokes.text: Andy Richter vs. his identical brother trying to get into the Ealing Club — 'It's my stupid brother! He looks just like me.'
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at the Ealing Club to find someone arguing they're already inside
jokes.punchline: It's Andy Richter, who has an identical brother who is already upstairs — 'he looks just like me' — a reference to the running AD joke about Andy having multiple identical brothers
jokes.explanation: The Andy Richter identical brothers gag is a solid Season 4 running joke. The bureaucratic confusion of being denied entry because your identical brother is already inside is perfectly AD-absurdist.

jokes.text: George Michael asks for a 'sea-breeze' at the bar — 'Make it a strong one. Put some stank on that.'
jokes.setup: George Michael girds himself for his meeting with Michael
jokes.punchline: 'Put some stank on that' — an attempt at street-cool phrasing applied to a sea breeze, one of the most innocuous cocktails; the affected toughness around a fruity drink is very George Michael
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'put some stank on that' with a sea-breeze order is a good character comedy beat — George Michael's attempt to perform toughness around a very gentle cocktail.

jokes.text: George Michael ordering at the bar: 'Hi. Yeah, just a sea-breeze, please. Make it a strong one. Put some stank on that.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is 'girding himself' for his first meeting with his father post-betrayal
jokes.punchline: His attempt at adult masculine drinking — ordering a sea breeze (a sweet, light cocktail) and demanding it be 'strong' with 'stank on it' — is the most George Michael possible drink order for the most un-threatening cocktail
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy — the sea breeze is the funniest possible choice of 'strong' drink. 'Put some stank on that' trying to add machismo to a sea breeze is a perfect George Michael 2.0 affectation. Could only be this character.

jokes.text: Rebel criticizes George Michael as a jerk: 'You're a bigger jerk than Andy Richter over there!' / Andy: 'Loosen up, bitch. I'm Andy Richter! I probably make like $8,000 a day or something.'
jokes.setup: Rebel is angry at George Michael for his blasé attitude
jokes.punchline: Andy Richter interjects from across the room with a non-sequitur about his income — 'I probably make like $8,000 a day or something' — defending himself from being used as a jerk benchmark
jokes.explanation: Andy Richter defending his honor from across the room by citing his approximate salary ('or something') is a great celebrity cameo beat. The uncertainty of 'probably' and 'or something' undercuts the braggadocio perfectly.

jokes.text: 'You were in the remake of "Dangerous Cousins." I signed up to Netflix because of that movie.'
jokes.setup: George Michael claims he knows who Rebel is
jokes.punchline: 'Dangerous Cousins' — a movie title that, in the context of George Michael's feelings for Maeby and the overall Bluth family dynamics, is deeply ironic; signing up for Netflix specifically for a movie called 'Dangerous Cousins' is also very George Michael
jokes.explanation: Exceptional layered joke. 'Dangerous Cousins' works on three levels: (1) it's what George Michael would be drawn to given his feelings for Maeby, (2) signing up for Netflix specifically for this movie is a meta-reference to AD's Netflix revival, (3) the title itself is a darkly comic summary of his entire arc. The character integration is near-perfect — only George Michael Bluth would tell a woman he signed up for Netflix for a movie called 'Dangerous Cousins.'

jokes.text: 'I prefer a woman who aims higher.' — George Michael's response to Rebel saying women must throw themselves at his feet
jokes.setup: Rebel says she imagines women throw themselves at George Maharis's feet
jokes.punchline: 'I prefer a woman who aims higher' — a confident, slightly flirtatious line that's both smooth and slightly cringe (it implies he wants to be aimed for... elsewhere)
jokes.explanation: One of George Michael's best seduction lines — it's confident, economical, and slightly dirty without being crude. Rebel's genuine laugh and 'That was good. Smooth.' reaction validates it as a successful moment. The fact that it actually works makes it funnier — his deliberate attempts fail, but this one-liner lands.

jokes.text: 'That was funny.' / 'Yeah, I know.' / 'That was good. That was...' / 'Smooth.' / 'Smooth. Hey, we have the same taste in words.'
jokes.setup: Rebel is impressed by George Michael's 'aims higher' line
jokes.punchline: George Michael says 'Yeah, I know' immediately — breaking the casual cool he just established — and then they both say 'smooth' at the same time, leading to 'we have the same taste in words' as a pickup line
jokes.explanation: 'Yeah, I know' immediately deflates the smooth moment, revealing George Michael's nervousness. 'We have the same taste in words' is charmingly awkward — a callback to 'we have the same taste in music' type openers, applied to a single shared word.

jokes.text: George Michael's father is paging 'George Michael' over the club intercom while George Michael is trying to pass himself off as 'George Maharis' — he sneaks away to 'check his messages in the men's room'
jokes.setup: George Michael is mid-seduction with Rebel under the alias George Maharis
jokes.punchline: His father is paging his real name over the intercom, forcing him to flee to the bathroom while pretending he just wants to 'check messages'
jokes.explanation: A solid cringe beat — the father interfering with the son's romantic moment is classic sitcom, but elevated here by the alias situation. The bathroom escape is the right physical comedy choice.

jokes.text: Michael and George Michael are leaving simultaneous voicemails for each other — Michael excitedly describing a plane crash traffic jam while George Michael hopes it keeps Michael away from the club
jokes.setup: Michael is stuck in traffic because of a plane crash on the freeway; George Michael is simultaneously leaving him a message hoping to avoid seeing him
jokes.punchline: Their voicemails become increasingly parallel — Michael describes the crash with excitement (filming it, 'Glad you're getting some footage') while George Michael hopes it keeps him occupied
jokes.explanation: The parallel voicemail structure is clever — both father and son are leaving increasingly elaborate messages simultaneously. Michael's excitement about the plane crash ('I'm going to video this and send it in to the news') reveals his characteristic emotional distance from tragedy when it doesn't affect him.

jokes.text: Michael's traffic jam monologue escalates to: 'Bet you 500 bucks they find a dead kid behind the stick. Or maybe on the dead dad's lap whose only fault was loving that kid too much and letting him fly the plane.'
jokes.setup: Michael is describing a plane crash on the freeway with increasing speculation
jokes.punchline: He invents an entire tragic narrative about a dead dad who let his kid fly the plane — betting $500 on it — which mirrors his own relationship with George Michael in a darkly ironic way
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional dark comedy with perfect character integration. Michael invents a narrative about a father who loved his kid 'too much' and let him take control — which is EXACTLY what Michael did with George Michael (projecting his ambitions, letting him 'steer'). The irony that Michael can see this pattern in a stranger but not in himself is devastatingly character-specific. 'Bet you 500 bucks they find a dead kid behind the stick' is a great dark-comedy line.

jokes.text: Andy Richter: 'Oh, yeah, sure. Whatever you want... it's on Andy.' / 'Thank you.' / 'It's on this rich a--hole. Bye.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Andy Richter to vouch for him at the club; Andy agrees magnanimously
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately undercuts his own generosity by telling someone else 'it's on this rich a--hole' while Michael is still there
jokes.explanation: Andy Richter's self-aware 'rich a--hole' line is a sharp celebrity cameo beat — he's both generous and immediately resentful. The instant deflation of his own niceness is funny.

jokes.text: Michael to Ron Howard: 'A month ago, I would have told you that he and I were exactly the same, that we were twins. But now, I wouldn't even think of my son as my brother.'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining his changed relationship with George Michael to Ron Howard
jokes.punchline: 'I wouldn't even think of my son as my brother' — an accidentally revealing statement that gestures toward the show's family dynamics (Michael treating George Michael as a peer/brother), and the irony that Ron Howard is the show's narrator/producer
jokes.explanation: A genuinely layered joke — Michael says 'I wouldn't think of my son as my brother' as a declaration of healthy boundaries, not realizing it reveals how brotherly/inappropriate his relationship with George Michael has been. Plus, Ron Howard being the narrator who knows everything makes this conversation meta.

jokes.text: The Fakeblock lawsuit: 'Was it or was it not my client who put up the initial seed money for the block software that served as the underlying architecture of Fakeblock?' / 'It was 99 cents.' / 'Times three.' / 'Times three.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is facing a lawsuit over Fakeblock ownership; P-Hound's lawyer argues he provided seed money
jokes.punchline: 'It was 99 cents. Times three.' — the 99-cents-times-three running gag gets its final legal-context callback, with multiple characters saying it in unison
jokes.explanation: The running gag reaches its apex in a legal context — the 99-cent investment being cited as 'seed money' in a Fakeblock lawsuit is a perfect comedic landing spot. Multiple characters saying 'times three' in unison is a great group-punchline payoff. The absurdity of a legal proceeding centering on $2.97 is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Can I say something to my client?' / (pause) / 'Take to the sea!'
jokes.setup: The lawyer asks to confer with his client in the Fakeblock lawsuit
jokes.punchline: 'Take to the sea!' — whatever the lawyer intended to say to his client, the advice is to flee by nautical means; completely non-sequitur legal advice delivered with apparent sincerity
jokes.explanation: The episode's final joke and a great one. 'Take to the sea!' delivered as urgent legal counsel is perfectly absurdist — the long pause before the advice builds anticipation, and the nautical non-sequitur pays it off completely. Three words, maximum absurdity. Extremely quotable and an excellent button for the episode. In the AD tradition of lawyers being hilariously bad (Barry Zuckerkorn).

tmdb_overview: At UC Irvine things get steamy when George-Michael finds himself in a love triangle with his best friend Ray and his girlfriend Becky.
insight: Season finale ties disparate threads with 75 jokes across absurdist callbacks and character-driven humor.
